meta.title: Pathology Informatics in the Age of AI

glossary.definition: Artificial Intelligence — in this context, large language models (LLMs) used as development assistants for code generation, design consultation, and review automation.
Application Programming Interface — a defined set of rules that allows different software systems to communicate with each other.
Epic's laboratory information system (LIS) module — manages specimen tracking, test ordering, and result reporting in clinical laboratories.
Standardized, repetitive code that must be written to set up frameworks, configurations, or structures before any meaningful logic can be added.
College of American Pathologists — the organization that accredits pathology laboratories in the U.S.
The total mental effort required to complete a task — in software development, the sum of remembering syntax, navigating frameworks, managing complexity, and making design decisions.
Data-Driven Documents — a JavaScript library for creating dynamic, interactive data visualizations in web browsers using SVG, HTML, and CSS.
Development + Security + Operations — a practice that integrates security into every phase of the software development lifecycle.
Design History File — the complete collection of documents that describe the design of a medical device/software, required by FDA.
A containerization platform that packages software with all its dependencies into portable containers, ensuring it runs identically on any machine.
Electronic Health Record — a digital version of a patient's medical chart, maintained by healthcare providers across encounters and settings.
A distributed, open-source search and analytics engine — widely used for log analytics, monitoring, and full-text search at enterprise scale.
Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources — a modern HL7 standard for exchanging healthcare data electronically using web-based APIs.
Failure Mode and Effects Analysis — a systematic method for identifying what could go wrong, how severe it would be, and how to prevent it.
A workflow need that is technically attainable but absent from vendor software — the central concept from Gershkovich & Sinard (2015).
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act — U.S. federal law governing the privacy and security of protected health information (PHI).
Health Level 7 — a set of international standards for transferring clinical and administrative data between hospital information systems.
Immunohistochemistry — a staining technique that uses antibodies to detect specific proteins in tissue sections.
JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable data format used to store and exchange structured data between systems and applications.
Laboratory Information System — software that manages clinical laboratory operations including specimen tracking, test ordering, result reporting, and quality control.
Large Language Model — an AI system trained on vast text and code corpora, capable of generating natural language, writing code, and reasoning across domains.
Next-Generation Sequencing — high-throughput DNA/RNA sequencing used in molecular pathology for variant detection.
A formal, structured representation of knowledge within a domain — defines concepts, categories, and the relationships between them (e.g., SNOMED CT in medicine).
Protected Health Information — any patient data covered by HIPAA privacy regulations.
Quality Management System — the organizational framework of policies, processes, and records that ensures product quality and regulatory compliance.
Requirements Traceability Matrix — a table linking every requirement to its test, proving nothing was missed.
Structured Query Language — the standard language for querying and managing data in relational databases.
User Requirements Specification — a document that defines what a system must do from the user's perspective, written in plain language.
Whole Slide Image — a high-resolution digital scan of a glass slide, often several gigabytes in size.

An open-source synthetic name generator that produces linguistically plausible names belonging to no real person — built for safe, HIPAA-compliant test data generation using syllabic mixing across seven languages.

slides.title: Pathology Informatics in the Age of AI
slides.subtitle: How Emerging Technologies Are Transforming the Tools We Build

slides.title: Conflict of Interest Disclosure
bullets: Consultant for Applicate Technologies Inc.
This presentation is intended solely for educational purposes. All content reflects the presenter's independent views and does not represent the official position of any institution or vendor.

slides.title: The Work Is Clinical — The Optimization Is Informatics
slides.subtitle: Every clinical action is an information event

cards.label: Case Assembly
cards.description: What belongs together — specimen, blocks, slides, stains, reports linked by accession

cards.label: Stain Ordering
cards.description: Rules + exceptions — reflex panels, conditional IHC, clinical context driving selection

cards.label: Report Distribution
cards.description: Who must know, when, by what channel — provider, registry, tumor board, patient portal

cards.label: Amendments
cards.description: Error detection + correction propagation — every downstream consumer must receive the update

takeaway.text: The clinical work is inseparable from information flow. Technology makes optimization possible; informatics makes it correct.
takeaway.comment: You don't 'do informatics' by clicking buttons. You face informatics problems whenever you have to reason about identity, meaning, and downstream consequences before you act. Informatics is how we redesign that information flow so the system carries more of the cognitive burden.

left.heading: Informatics
bullets: Designs information structures + workflows to improve outcomes
Optimizes the "human + system" loop
Clinical logic made operational

right.heading: IT
bullets: Operates infrastructure, security, devices, networks
Installs, maintains, ensures uptime
Keeps the plumbing running

takeaway.text: Informatics = clinical logic made operational. IT = infrastructure. Both essential — different jobs.
takeaway.comment: When a reflex stain doesn't fire correctly, that's an informatics problem (logic). When the network is down, that's IT (infrastructure). Conflating the two leads to the wrong people solving the wrong problems.

slides.title: Thesis: The Metaphor Became Practical
slides.subtitle: "Software should be an immediate result of your thought" — once aspirational, now operational (Lincoln & Korpman, 1980)

left.heading: The Enduring Challenge
bullets: Pathology informatics is built on logical sciences — representation, inference, decision, system behavior
The hardest problems are semantic: entities (case/part/block/slide), time, provenance, constraints
These require domain expertise — the kind pathologists already have

right.heading: What AI Changed
bullets: Not what needs to be built — the economics of building it
The gap between "I know what this should do" and "it exists" collapsed
~100× reduction in effort to reach the same regulated quality bar

takeaway.text: The bottleneck was never the idea — it was the cost of implementation. That cost just dropped by two orders of magnitude.
takeaway.comment: This lecture is about what that collapse means for pathology: the tools you've wished for, the integrations you've worked around, the dashboards you've sketched on napkins — they are now within reach.

slides.speakerNotes: Five objectives that build on each other: (1) Differentiate informatics from IT — foundational. (2) Identify the functionality gap — the problem. (3) Explain how AI changes the economics — the transformation. (4) Evaluate evidence from real tools — proof. (5) Describe a practical pathway — action. The progression is deliberate: you can't evaluate evidence without understanding what problem it solves, and you can't act without understanding the economics.

slides.title: What "Workflow" Means in the Lab
slides.subtitle: The chain of tasks and events that creates clinical value — the substrate everything else depends on

cards.label: Workflow
cards.description: Why your day feels smooth or chaotic — the foundation for data, automation, and AI

cards.label: Good Design
cards.description: Cases flow, handoffs happen, exceptions surface — you don't notice it working

cards.label: Bad Design
cards.description: Constant interruptions, manual tracking, missing information, cognitive overload

takeaway.text: Workflow is the substrate on which everything else (data, automation, AI) depends.
takeaway.comment: You cannot layer AI on broken workflow. If case assembly is chaotic, an AI that prioritizes your worklist will just shuffle chaos faster. Fix the substrate first.

slides.title: Anatomy of a Delay — Where the Model Breaks

steps.label: Collection
steps.detail: Specimen obtained in the OR or procedure suite; fixation starts the clock.

steps.label: Transport
steps.detail: Courier or pneumatic tube. Every handoff is an identity-check opportunity.

steps.label: Accessioning
steps.detail: Specimen registered in LIS, barcoded, assigned to a case.

steps.label: Grossing
steps.detail: Pathologist or PA describes and sections the specimen; selects blocks.

steps.label: Processing
steps.detail: Overnight tissue processor: dehydration, clearing, paraffin infiltration. Cannot be accelerated without compromise.

steps.label: Slide prep
steps.detail: Embedding, microtomy, staining (H&E ± IHC). Quality depends on block orientation.

steps.label: Delivery
steps.delayNote: Slides sat in tray — no pickup alert
steps.detail: Slides complete but no system event fires. Pathologist unaware they are ready. Sticky-note workflow fails silently.

steps.label: Path. receipt
steps.detail: Slides arrive at pathologist's desk or scanner. Digital case assembly begins.

steps.label: Review
steps.detail: Pathologist examines slides. Finds diagnostic ambiguity requiring deeper section.

steps.label: Reorder
steps.detail: Recut request propagates back to histology. No system models this as a rework cycle — TAT metrics undercount the true cost.

steps.label: Re-processing
steps.detail: Block retrieved, additional sections cut, stained, re-delivered. Partial restart of the analytic phase.

steps.label: Sign-out
steps.detail: Diagnosis finalized and report authored. Clinical content is the shortest step in the timeline.

steps.label: Distribution
steps.detail: Report transmitted to EHR, registry, referring clinician. Final handoff.

takeaway.text: Calling this a 'pre-analytical delay' tells you nothing. The delay lives in a specific handoff between specific steps — and the fix depends entirely on which one.
takeaway.comment: Was it a distribution failure? A reorder? A grossing bottleneck? Each demands a different intervention. You need step-level granularity, not phase-level labels.

slides.title: The "Gap": Why Workarounds Appear
slides.subtitle: Requirements evolve; closed systems don't → gap → workarounds. Each one is system design evidence.

cards.label: Shadow Spreadsheets
cards.description: Cases tracked outside the LIS because the LIS can't show what you need to see

cards.label: "Ignore the Status"
cards.description: LIS status labels that are administratively neat but clinically false

cards.label: Copy-Paste
cards.description: Manual transcription between systems — each handoff is an error opportunity

cards.label: Human Integration
cards.description: People becoming the bus between systems that don't talk to each other

cards.label: Sticky Notes
cards.description: Monitors decorated with the workflow logic the system was supposed to encode

takeaway.text: Workarounds are not "user failure" — they're system design evidence.
takeaway.comment: Every sticky note on a monitor, every shared spreadsheet tracking cases 'outside the system,' every 'please ignore this status' message is a requirements document written in frustration. Catalog them — they tell you exactly what the system fails to support.

slides.title: The Functionality Gap
slides.subtitle: Important + technically attainable + absent from vendor = the gap where custom software lives (Gershkovich & Sinard, 2015)

cards.label: LIS Design Intent
cards.description: Your LIS was designed for billing and compliance — not for your clinical workflow

cards.label: Workaround Risk
cards.description: When the gap exists, workarounds fill it — and workarounds are where errors live

cards.label: Interoperability
cards.description: of labs report interoperability as biggest obstacle (Lukić, 2017)

cards.label: AI-Assisted Fix
cards.description: Close many gaps quickly with AI-assisted development — without giving up validation

takeaway.text: A 'functionality gap' = important + technically attainable + absent from vendor. LIS solves the general case; your lab is a specific case.
takeaway.comment: This isn't anti-vendor. Vendors build essential infrastructure. But they can't know that your frozen section service needs a real-time OR notification, or that your molecular team needs a custom NGS reporting pipeline. That gap is where custom software lives.

slides.title: Poll: What Frustrates You Most?
slides.prompt: Which workflow gap causes the most friction in your daily practice?
options: Case assembly / missing slides or reports
Alert fatigue or missing critical notifications
Poor integration between systems (LIS, PACS, EHR)
Lack of real-time dashboards or analytics
slides.note: We'll return to these pain points when we discuss what AI makes possible.

slides.title: Case: The Positive Control You Can't See
slides.scenario: You're signing out an IHC case in your office — a quarter mile from the histology lab. The CK7 stain looks weak. Was it the tissue or the reagent? The positive control would tell you, but it's one physical slide shared across the entire staining batch. It's somewhere in the lab. Nobody digitized it. The LIS doesn't even know it exists. How do you verify stain validity without leaving your desk?
options: A. Walk to the lab, find the batch, pull the control slide, walk back
B. Call the histotech and ask them to check it for you
C. Digitize the positive control and link it to the batch in software
D. Trust the stain and sign it out — the lab would have flagged a failure
discussion: No commercial LIS associates positive controls with staining batches — this functionality simply does not exist
One positive control validates an entire batch: if it works, every slide in that batch is confirmed
At Yale, we built custom software that creates staining batches, scans positive controls, and links them digitally
This is a real 'functionality gap' that no vendor has addressed (Gershkovich & Sinard, 2015)
slides.discussionFrame: This isn't hypothetical — we solved this at Yale because no vendor would. Custom software turned a daily frustration into a one-click verification.

slides.title: When You Hit a Gap: The Seven Options
slides.subtitle: From Gershkovich & Sinard (2015) — a decision framework for closing functionality gaps

takeaway.text: Most labs default to options 1–3. The labs that transform choose 5–7.
takeaway.comment: Ignoring the gap or patching manually is the path of least resistance — but it compounds. Every manual workaround is a future error waiting to happen. AI-assisted development just made options 5–7 accessible to labs that never had a development team.

left.heading: Traditional LIS Tracking
bullets: Log what happened (timestamps, statuses)
Passive — waits for humans to check
Metrics after the fact (TAT reports)
Errors discovered downstream
"The system recorded it"

right.heading: Workflow Orchestration
bullets: Route work to the right person at the right time
Surface exceptions as they happen — not after
Coordinate handoffs across teams and instruments
Enforce constraints that prevent known failure modes
Adapt — dynamic prioritization, load-aware assignment

takeaway.text: A good system doesn't just record your workflow — it actively drives it. Orchestration means the system carries cognitive burden so you don't have to.
takeaway.comment: Most LIS deployments are still at the tracking level. The gap between these two columns is where most operational pain lives — and where AI-assisted development can help build the bridge.

takeaway.text: Software should be an immediate result of your thought. That used to be a metaphor. Now it is practical.
takeaway.comment: The earliest clinical and laboratory systems emerged from exactly this principle. Pioneers automated their own workflows, not someone else's idea of a workflow. AI has made that mode of building accessible again — this time to anyone with domain expertise.

slides.title: Two Orders of Magnitude
slides.subtitle: What AI changed is not what we build or how we review it — it's the cost of satisfying quality standards

cards.label: Quality Gates
cards.description: Requirements, design controls, verification, traceability, audit trail — same rigor as before

cards.label: Effort Reduction
cards.description: Work that took a team months now takes one informaticist weeks

cards.label: Supplementary Tools
cards.description: Technologies that were never worth building now emerge naturally from the work

cards.label: Foundation
cards.description: AI amplified a methodology that already worked — build the discipline before the tooling

takeaway.text: AI did not create our methodology. It amplified a methodology that already worked.
takeaway.comment: This is the crucial distinction: AI amplified a practice that already existed. Organizations that try to use AI without the underlying discipline will just produce bugs faster. The sequencing matters — build the culture first.

slides.title: Cognitive Load Redistribution
slides.subtitle: Where developer effort concentrates — a structural model informed by industry research

takeaway.text: Freeing up cognitive load is only valuable if you have something worth thinking about.
takeaway.comment: Think of every time you've looked up how to configure a system you've configured forty times before. That look-up is not thinking. AI absorbs it. What remains (should remain) is the decision about whether it's the right tool at all.

slides.title: Compression of the Feedback Loop
slides.subtitle: The speed change is not linear — it changes the character of design decisions

takeaway.text: Speed doesn't just change how fast you work. It changes how you think.
takeaway.comment: When a design decision takes two hours to implement and test, you commit to fewer of them. Compress the loop and the economics change. You can prototype three approaches before lunch and choose the best one.

slides.title: The Blank Page Problem
slides.subtitle: Two metaphors for the same terror — and what dissolved it

takeaway.text: You traded the blank page for a plausible wrong answer. Learn to tell the difference.
takeaway.comment: Every developer — and every pathologist who's tried to build a tool — knows the void of the empty file. AI didn't fill the void. It made the first mark free. The work shifted from generation to judgment.

slides.speakerNotes: AI can use codified knowledge: rules, dictionaries, ontologies, interface specifications. It cannot inherit lived experience, tacit judgment, or institutional memory. What matters in pathology is often knowing which ambiguity is dangerous, which exception matters, and when a formally correct result is operationally wrong. The pathologist with deep domain knowledge and AI assistance is not slightly better than one without AI. The gap is qualitative. In pathology informatics, you ARE the domain expert. The reflex rules, the edge cases in specimen accessioning, the clinical context that makes a report actionable — that knowledge is yours. AI handles the implementation. You provide the judgment.

AI accelerates implementation. AI does not clarify intent. The decision about what to build — the right data model, the right decomposition, the right clinical logic — is unchanged. Vague requirements now produce working code that does the wrong thing. In pathology: if you can't precisely define what 'case ready for sign-out' means in your lab, AI will build you a beautiful system that gets it wrong. Precision of thought is no longer optional. The fork on this slide is real: clear intent produces the right system at scale. Vague intent produces working code for the wrong problem. AI will produce correct, compiling, well-formatted code for the wrong problem. Faster than ever.

slides.title: What We Build — The Operational Landscape
slides.subtitle: REALM: Rapidly Evolving Agile Laboratory Modules — 19 years of systems across the continuum of care

slides.title: The Problem: Names That Don't Exist
slides.subtitle: Case Study — Xenonym · github.com/OpenPathologyFoundation/xenonym

left.heading: The Context
bullets: You are building a system that handles patient data
You need realistic test data that exercises schema, validation, and integration
You cannot use real patients — HIPAA is not a suggestion
Obviously fake data (John Doe) tests almost nothing

right.heading: The Requirement
bullets: Names that sound like names but belong to no one
Names that break systems — NULL, DROP, diacritics
Names from diverse linguistic origins
A tool that was never worth building before AI collapsed the cost

takeaway.text: Obviously fake data tests almost nothing. Real data is irregular, surprising, and occasionally adversarial.
takeaway.comment: The boring solution is to use obviously fake names — John Doe, Jane Smith. But that tests almost nothing, because real data is irregular, surprising, and occasionally adversarial.

slides.speakerNotes: The design challenge: How do you generate names that feel linguistically plausible but are structurally foreign to any single language? The solution AI surfaced: syllabic mixing across seven languages — Finnish, Georgian, Yoruba, Japanese, Welsh, Swahili, Hungarian. The probability that the combination is a real name: vanishingly small. The probability that it sounds like a name to a human ear: high. I did not search for this solution. I described the constraint and the model surfaced an approach from computational linguistics that I would not have reached independently. This is AI functioning as a cross-domain consultant — exactly the mode that matters for pathology informatics, where our problems span medicine, computer science, and operations.

takeaway.text: The whole system is publicly accessible, costs nothing, and was built to solve a real problem — not to demonstrate a capability.
takeaway.comment: Xenonym generates HIPAA-safe synthetic patient data including adversarial edge cases: names containing NULL, DROP, DELETE (SQL injection test cases), diacritics (Müller, Čapek, Ñoño). If your system can't store 'Nontra Null,' that's a bug — and depending on context, a compliance failure.

slides.title: IBIS — Natural-Language Search Over Pathology Data

left.heading: What It Does
bullets: Clinician or researcher asks a natural-language question
IBIS turns it into a query over both structured and unstructured pathology data
Returns a cohort in seconds, not weeks
No SQL, no analyst queue, no manual chart review

right.heading: How It Was Built
bullets: Built outside Yale on entirely synthetic data
Docker-based Elasticsearch stack — never touched PHI
Pathway in: open source → adapt to internal data model → Enterprise GitHub → SPA review → CI/CD
AI-assisted development made this feasible for one informaticist

takeaway.text: IBIS illustrates the thesis: a tool that was previously a team-sized project is now buildable by one domain expert with AI assistance.
takeaway.comment: The honest version: it would have been better to build this inside the institution from the start. The constraint was institutional, not technical. The methodology, the people, the discipline — already existed.

slides.title: WBC ΔΣ: Small App, Complete System
slides.subtitle: A manual differential counter — rebuilt from a broken legacy app in a single AI-assisted session. mdc.openpathology.org

cards.label: Interface
cards.description: Undo, thresholds, comments, templates, clipboard, session history — zero mouse dependency

cards.label: Data Handling
cards.description: No server-side patient data — sessionStorage only, nothing transmitted

cards.label: QMS / DHF
cards.description: URS (47 requirements), SRS (88 specs), Risk Analysis, Traceability Matrix — per 21 CFR 820

cards.label: Automated Tests
cards.description: 40 test suites, 0 npm dependencies, 100% pass rate in 78ms

cards.label: Traceability
cards.description: Every test → SRS requirement → FMEA hazard → VV protocol vector

cards.label: Dependencies
cards.description: Vanilla JS — no frameworks, no npm install for testing, minimal attack surface

takeaway.text: The code works AND it's defensible. That's the new standard: AI speeds implementation, artifacts lock safety.
takeaway.comment: Live at mdc.openpathology.org — open-source at github.com/OpenPathologyFoundation/tessera. The key lesson: AI generated the scaffold. A pathologist defined what 'safe and correct' means.

slides.title: What This Presentation Actually Is
slides.subtitle: What you have been looking at for the last 40 minutes:

takeaway.text: There is no PowerPoint here. This deck is a JSON document rendered through a browser application — version-controlled, diff-able, reproducible.
takeaway.comment: The medium is the argument. This presentation was built the way we build clinical tools, and it took hours, not days. Every visualization is generated from data, not drawn by hand. The entire lecture is a single JSON file.

left.heading: Fast Path (AI Helps Here)
bullets: Generate UI scaffold and layout
Wire events, handlers, state management
Generate test skeletons from requirements
Draft QMS documentation structure
Debug and fix tooling/build issues

right.heading: Clinical Control (Humans Own)
bullets: URS: what must be true clinically
SRS: testable system behavior
Risk analysis + mitigations (FMEA)
Verification vectors + regression tests
Design direction and domain decisions

takeaway.text: AI is a development partner. The human provides clinical domain expertise and design direction. AI handles execution at scale.
takeaway.comment: This is the practical version of the governing principle: you define what 'correct' means, AI builds it, you verify it. The safety envelope is locked by humans + automated tests — never by AI alone.

left.heading: AI Does (Development Partner)
bullets: Generate UI scaffold and layout from specs
Write boilerplate, event handlers, state management
Draft test skeletons from requirements
Surface cross-domain techniques (e.g. computational linguistics)
Debug, refactor, and fix tooling issues

right.heading: AI Must NOT (Non-Negotiable)
bullets: No autonomous clinical decisions — AI assists, humans diagnose
No PHI in prompts — patient data never enters AI context
No hidden transformations — every state change must be testable
No unverified production code — tested + traced before deploy

takeaway.text: AI is a development tool, not a clinical decision-maker. The safety envelope is locked by humans + automated tests.
takeaway.comment: Every AI risk has a testable mitigation. Hallucinated logic → unit tests. Prompt drift → regression suites. Data leakage → no-PHI policy. Supply chain risk → minimal dependencies.

slides.title: AI Governance: Who Validates, Who Is Liable?

left.heading: The Questions
bullets: On-label vs off-label: using AI outside its approved indication changes the risk profile
Bias: training data composition determines performance across demographics and tissue types
Documentation: when AI contributes, what goes in the record?

right.heading: The Principles
bullets: Validation: vendor claims ≠ institutional validation — you must verify independently
Stewardship extends: AI governance is the natural extension of lab information stewardship
Same framework: requirements → tests → traceability applies to AI tools too

slides.speakerNotes: Three observations from years of building with AI — for pathologists. (1) Domain knowledge — the person who knows what a diagnostic clause means, what the institution's history is, what the workflow requires — is MORE valuable when AI handles the generic. You are that person. (2) Architectural clarity — vague requirements now produce working code that does the wrong thing. The cost of imprecision went UP. When you write a URS, precision matters more than ever. (3) The human in the loop — not the model, not the tool. The pathologist who knows what they're building and why. All three converge: the differentiator was always you. AI made human judgment more consequential, not less necessary.

epigraph.text: You don't need to be a developer. You need to be the person who understands the problem.

steps.question: Spot gaps
steps.detail: Every workaround is a requirements document waiting to be written.

steps.question: Write specs
steps.detail: URS-style requirements with clinical rationales — the highest-leverage step.

steps.question: Prototype
steps.detail: Describe the problem precisely; let AI scaffold code against your spec.

steps.question: Test
steps.detail: Be the clinical validator — your adversarial edge cases are gold.

steps.question: Advocate
steps.detail: Present the gap, the solution, and the evidence to leadership.

takeaway.text: The new superpower: a 1-page spec, an AI-generated scaffold, and tests that prove safety.
takeaway.comment: The bottleneck is no longer coding — it's domain clarity. And that's what you already have.

slides.speakerNotes: Five actions arranged on a commitment timeline: today you can spot gaps. This week you can write the spec. This month you can prototype with AI. This quarter you can validate. This year you can advocate. Each step is small; together they change your role from consumer of software to architect of it.

takeaway.text: The new superpower: write a 1-page spec, generate a scaffold with AI, and prove safety via tests + QMS documents.
takeaway.comment: The bottleneck is no longer coding — it's domain clarity. And that's what you already have.

slides.title: The Question Returns
bullets:
The question was never
"What can AI build?"

The question is
"What workflow problem do you understand deeply enough
to describe precisely — and recognize when it's solved?"

takeaway.text: The clarity is the work.
takeaway.comment: If the answer is yes, everything in this talk is an accelerant. The tools exist. The methodology exists. The cost barrier is gone. What remains is your expertise and your willingness to act on it.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
items: Informatics is the logic of clinical work expressed in software — the abstraction layer that matters is workflow, not hardware.
The functionality gap is real: your LIS solves the general case; your lab is a specific case. Workarounds are where errors live.
AI collapsed the cost of building by ~100×. Same quality bar, same validation rigor — radically less effort.
The evidence is concrete: Xenonym (synthetic data), IBIS (natural-language search), WBC ΔΣ (clinical-grade counting), and this presentation (JSON-driven, no PowerPoint).
You already have the hardest skill — domain expertise. AI handles the syntax. Your job is to define what 'safe and correct' means.

cta.label: Your challenge:
cta.text: Identify one workflow gap in your practice. Write a one-page spec with five requirements — each with a clinical rationale — plus three plausible failure modes. That document is more valuable than any code.

slides.title: Questions & Discussion
slides.contact:
Peter Gershkovich, MD. MHA
Yale University School of Medicine

A question to start:
"What is the one workflow problem you understand deeply enough to describe precisely — and would build a tool for if you could?"

xenonym.openpathology.org · mdc.openpathology.org
